corrected_fields.macro_context: Climate-related shocks function as macro catalysts that propagate through food and energy channels, generating inflationary pressures and output contractions. The paper's BIS-MS model simulations suggest these shocks can require policy rate adjustments, implying climate events can shift the monetary policy calculus. Economies highly exposed to agricultural and energy sectors—including several African countries—face amplified effects due to greater sectoral exposure and limited fiscal/insurance buffers.
corrected_fields.market_structure_context: While the paper focuses on macroeconomic transmission channels, its findings have market structure implications: sovereign bond yields in climate-exposed economies face elevated risk premiums, commodity markets (especially agricultural and energy) exhibit heightened volatility around climate shock events, and central bank frameworks incorporating climate risk may shift expected policy reaction functions.
